mnemonics.title: VC vs Datagram Differences
mnemonics.content:
VC = CONNECTED (Connection setup, In-order delivery, Short VCI, QoS support, Entire path fails if one link fails)

DG = DISCONNECTED (No setup, Disorder possible, Full address needed, No QoS, Rerouting possible)

mnemonics.title: Three Phases of Virtual Circuit
mnemonics.content: CED: Connection establishment → Data transfer → Disconnection/Teardown

acronyms.usage: A short label used to identify a virtual circuit at each router hop, typically 16 bits in Frame Relay or ATM
In ATM networks, VPI identifies a bundle of virtual circuits (virtual path) for efficient routing
Refers to the performance guarantees (bandwidth, latency, jitter) that can be provided by a network, more easily achieved with VC switching
A pre-configured virtual circuit that always exists, commonly used in Frame Relay and ATM networks
A virtual circuit that is dynamically established on demand, similar to a phone call

visualTips.visualization: Draw a straight line from source to destination with 3 routers in between. Label the path 'VC'. Show VCI values (like 10→20→30→40) changing at each router.
Draw source sending 3 packets to destination via 3 routers, but show each packet taking a different colored path. Packets arrive out of order (3, 1, 2).
Show a packet entering router with VCI=5, and exiting with VCI=15. This represents the 'label swapping' operation in VC switching.

analogies.analogy: Like making a train reservation - you book a specific route/seats before boarding. Your journey follows a predetermined path.
Like sending letters through postal service - each letter is routed independently based on the address. Different letters may take different routes.
Like a train ticket number - it identifies your specific booking and is checked/validated at each station (router) during the journey.
Like checking out of a hotel - you settle accounts and the room (resources) becomes available for other guests (new connections).
